name: Donna Miller
bio: Donna Miller (née Black; born September 7, 1965) is an American politician serving as Cook County Commissioner for the 6th District since December 2018, now running for the open IL-02 Congressional seat vacated by retiring Rep. Robin Kelly. She was born Donna Lynne Black on Chicago's South Side, attended Ogden Elementary, and graduated from Lane Technical High School in 1983. She earned a BBA from Howard University in 1987 and took graduate business courses at Pepperdine University in 1992. She adopted the surname Miller in 2001 when she married Dr. David E. Miller, a former Illinois state representative who served a decade in the General Assembly. They live in Lynwood, Illinois, with their two sons Daniel and Donovan. Her professional background spans 25+ years as a healthcare consultant and account specialist in the biotech/pharmaceutical industry. She has served on the board of Planned Parenthood Illinois (2012–present, board chair 2017–2018), Planned Parenthood Illinois Action (board chair 2015–2017), Christian Community Health Center, and worked with Congresswoman Robin Kelly's 2nd Congressional District Healthcare Task Force. She was director of Multi Cultural Markets with the American Heart Association. She served as vice-president of Illinois Democratic Women and was a member of the DNC Rules Committee for the 2016 Democratic National Convention. She supported Hillary Clinton's 2016 presidential campaign as part of Illinois Clinton Leadership Team. She ran unsuccessfully for Illinois State Senate (15th District) in 2012, placing second behind Napoleon Harris. In 2018 she won the Democratic primary for Cook County Commissioner District 6, defeating Patricia Joan Murphy and Crestwood Mayor Lou Presta, then won the general election unopposed. Re-elected to a second term in 2022. The 6th District covers south suburban Cook County including Lynwood, South Holland, Hazel Crest, and approximately 300,000+ residents across 36 municipalities. She is stepping down from the county seat (not seeking re-election) to run for Congress. Her 2026 congressional race has been dominated by controversy over her massive AIPAC-linked out-of-state donor network, which funded 87% of her Q4 2025 fundraising.
prior_office: Cook County Board Commissioner, District 6 (December 2018–present; stepping down to run for Congress)

jacket.note: FEC-reported total. 87% of Q4 2025 fundraising came from out-of-state donors per Hyde Park Herald analysis.

donors.name: Jeremy Pava
donors.note: Previously gave at least $283,000 to AIPAC/United Democracy Project (2023–2025). Max contribution to Miller. Also gave max to Fine and Bean.

donors.name: Ann Pava
donors.note: Spouse of Jeremy Pava. Gave maximum donation mid-December 2025. AIPAC-linked donor.

donors.name: Jerry Bednyak
donors.note: In-state donor. Has given at least $1.1M to AIPAC and United Democracy Project. Max contribution to Miller.

donors.name: AIPAC-linked donor network (258+ donors)
donors.note: At least 258 individual donors who previously gave to AIPAC/United Democracy Project contributed ~$470K+ to Miller in Q4 2025, representing 36%+ of total fundraising. Not a single entity — FEC-documented via donor overlap analysis.

donors.name: Affordable Chicago Now (Super PAC)
donors.note: Pop-up super PAC organized January 2026 running TV ads supporting Miller. Donors currently undisclosed. Identified by The American Prospect as part of AIPAC stealth infrastructure.

red_flags.label: AIPAC-Linked Out-of-State Donor Network Funds 87% of Q4 2025 Fundraising
red_flags.detail: 87% of Miller's Q4 2025 fundraising (~$892K) came from out-of-state donors. At least 258 of her donors previously gave to AIPAC/United Democracy Project. A super PAC called 'Affordable Chicago Now' (donors undisclosed) is running ads in her support, identified by The American Prospect as AIPAC stealth infrastructure. Miller's campaign denied soliciting AIPAC support but acknowledged accepting donations from known AIPAC contributors. This is a deeply progressive D+18 district where AIPAC-linked funding represents a significant electoral red flag.

red_flags.label: Stepping Down from County Seat Mid-Term to Run for Congress
red_flags.detail: Miller was elected to a second term as Cook County Commissioner in 2022 but is choosing not to finish her term, instead running for Congress. The Chicago Tribune's March 14, 2026 coverage confirmed she is among those vacating their county seats mid-cycle, opening the 6th District to a new race. The 6th District primary attracted 5 candidates, with attorney Wesam Shahed emerging as the frontrunner.

key_votes.bill: Cook County CPR/AED Training Program for County Employees (Miller's legislation)
key_votes.summary: Miller authored legislation creating a virtual countywide CPR/AED training program for all newly hired and current Cook County employees. The program won a National Achievement Award from NACO (National Association of Counties). Source: Miller's own 2022 WTTW campaign statement.

key_votes.bill: COVID-19 Federal Funding Channeling to Suburban Cook County
key_votes.summary: During the pandemic, Miller led efforts to channel federal COVID relief, FEMA funding, and first responder resources toward suburban Cook County communities in the 6th District. She coordinated food drives with USDA and local agencies to provide food for thousands of families, and secured funding to keep small businesses open.

key_votes.bill: Cook County Board — Membership on key oversight committees
key_votes.summary: Miller serves as Chair of Asset Management, Contract Compliance, Veterans, and Metra Commissioner Appointments-Zone 4 committees. She is Vice-Chair of Workforce, Housing and Community Development. This committee portfolio covers county infrastructure management, contractor accountability, transportation, and veteran services.

policy_platform.position: Advocate for eliminating healthcare disparities, particularly in the Southland/south suburban Cook County. Supports expanding Medicaid access, addressing CCHHS billing inefficiencies, and sharing charitable care burden more broadly across private hospitals in Cook County.

Identifies access to mental health services as the most pressing issue for constituents, particularly in South Cook County which scores among the highest on the Social Vulnerability Index. Advocates for expanded behavioral health services following closure of mental health clinics.

Long-standing supporter of women's reproductive rights. Former Chair of Planned Parenthood Illinois board (2017–2018) and Planned Parenthood Illinois Action (2015–2017). Ran for county commissioner in 2018 with Planned Parenthood support.

policy_platform.topic: Small Business / Economic Development in Southland
policy_platform.position: Advocates for investment in small businesses in south suburban Cook County. Secured pandemic funding for small businesses, coordinates across 36 municipalities in district.
Has not made specific property tax pledges in available sources. Candidate statement focused on healthcare and small business support.

career_history.highlight: Won 2018 primary beating Murphy and Presta; won 2022 re-election. Committees chaired: Asset Management, Contract Compliance, Veterans, Metra Zone 4. Stepping down to run for IL-02 Congress.

25+ years in biotech/pharmaceutical healthcare industry. Reviewed evidence-based medicine, peer-reviewed clinical and pharmacoeconomic data. This background was foundational to her COVID-19 pandemic response work as commissioner.

career_history.org: Alliance of Illinois State Dental Society

career_history.highlight: Member of DNC Rules Committee for the 2016 Democratic National Convention.
Worked with Congresswoman Robin Kelly; co-chaired Robin Kelly Annual Golf Event 2014–2017.

endorsements.significance: Endorsed Miller in her initial 2018 Cook County Commissioner race
Endorsed Miller in her initial 2018 Cook County Commissioner race

endorsements.org: United Food and Commercial Workers (2018 primary)

endorsements.significance: Rep. Kelly's endorsement of Miller in 2018 — note: Kelly is now the source of the open IL-02 seat
Schakowsky endorsed Miller for county in 2018 (Schakowsky is separately backing Biss for her own IL-09 seat in 2026)

endorsements.org: National Association of Social Workers — Illinois Council (2018 primary)

social_pulse.summary: Miller has relatively low organic social media presence but her IL-02 campaign has generated significant negative coverage around AIPAC-linked donor network (Hyde Park Herald, American Prospect). The out-of-state donor controversy (87% Q4 2025 funding from outside Illinois) is the dominant narrative. County board work has been below radar — no major controversies or viral moments. Viewed by progressives as a moderate-to-conservative Democrat backed by national money in a deeply progressive district.
social_pulse.sentiment: mixed-to-negative (among primary progressive voters due to AIPAC funding)
